**The Tale of the Watching Eyes**

In the beginning, humans wandered in small bands, knowing only the spirits of trees and streams—silent witnesses who demanded nothing. But as the bands grew into villages, whispers arose of greater beings: ancestors who saw theft in darkness, sky-watchers who counted every broken promise.

Those who proclaimed these watchful gods proved their devotion through ordeals—walking on fire, giving away wealth, scarring their flesh. Others saw their conviction and believed. The faithful gathered for dances that bent the mind, chants that merged individual into collective, festivals that bound them tighter than kin.

These communities swelled with children, for the watching gods blessed large families and condemned those who shirked duty. When drought came or enemies threatened, groups with stricter gods and fiercer rituals survived while others scattered.

The survivors spread across valleys and mountains, and others, seeing their success, copied their gods and ceremonies. Each generation added new powers to their deities, new reasons for their rituals—beliefs that began as accidents of the mind were sculpted by centuries of competition into instruments of cooperation.

Thus the moralizing gods conquered the world, not by divine decree, but through the slow arithmetic of fertility, faith, and endless human striving.
